question: As a business expands and the number of transactions grows large, why is the journal sub-divided into special journals?
options: To completely replace the ledger books of accounts
For quick, efficient, and accurate recording of repetitive business transactions
To convert cash transactions into credit transactions automatically
To avoid compliance with standard taxation auditing rules
explanation: As a business expands, journalizing every single transaction in one book becomes cumbersome. Sub-dividing the journal into special journals allows quick, efficient, and accurate recording of repetitive transactions of a similar nature.
subject: Accountancy

question: What are special journals meant for recording transactions of a similar nature alternative commonly called?
options: Ledger folios
Daybooks or subsidiary books
Voucher files
Trial statements sheet
explanation: Special journals, each meant for recording all transactions of a similar nature (such as all cash transactions or all credit purchases), are also called daybooks or subsidiary books.
subject: Accountancy

question: Where are business transactions recorded if they cannot be entered into any of the special subsidiary journals?
options: In the Cash Book
In the Purchases Book
In a journal called the Journal Proper
They are omitted from the accounts completely
explanation: Transactions that cannot be recorded in any special journal or subsidiary daybook are recorded in a separate sub-divided journal called the Journal Proper.
subject: Accountancy

question: Which of the following is an explicit example of a special purpose subsidiary book?
options: Ledger book
Trial Balance
Cash Book
Balance Sheet
explanation: The sub-division of the journal includes special purpose books or daybooks, the most common of which is the Cash Book (meant for recording all cash transactions).
subject: Accountancy

question: Which specialized book serves the dual purpose of both a book of original entry (journal) as well as a principal ledger account?
options: Purchases Book
Sales Book
Cash Book
Journal Proper
explanation: The Cash Book serves the purpose of both a journal as well as the ledger (cash) account. It is a book of original entry, and when it is maintained, no separate cash account is required in the ledger.
subject: Accountancy

question: What types of transactions are systematically recorded inside a standard Cash Book?
options: All credit sales and purchases transactions of goods
All transactions relating to cash receipts and cash payments
Only the adjustment and rectification entries row
Only the depreciation calculations of fixed assets
explanation: A Cash Book is a book of original entry in which all transactions relating to cash receipts and cash payments are recorded systematically.
subject: Accountancy

question: When an organization maintains a full Cash Book, what separate account is no longer required inside its ledger book?
options: Capital account
Purchases account
Cash or bank account
Creditors accounts
explanation: When a Cash Book is maintained, transactions of cash are not recorded in the journal, and no separate account for cash or bank is required in the ledger because the Cash Book itself serves that purpose.
subject: Accountancy

question: On what periodic interval basis is a standard Cash Book generally compiled and balanced in most organizations?
options: On a weekly basis strictly
On a monthly basis
Once every financial year only
On a shifting multi-year sequence
explanation: The Cash Book starts with the cash or bank balances at the beginning of the period. Generally, it is made and balanced on a monthly basis.
subject: Accountancy

question: What does a Single Column Cash Book track in chronological order?
options: Only bank transactions affected via cheques
A complete record of cash receipts and cash payments
Credit transactions separated by trade discounts
Only non-cash adjustments row columns
explanation: The single column cash book records all cash transactions of the business in a chronological order, serving as a complete record of cash receipts and cash payments.
subject: Accountancy

question: In a standard T-shaped layout Cash Book format, which side is designated for entering receipts?
options: The right-hand (Credit) side
The left-hand (Debit) side
The bottom summary footnote
Either side interchangeably depending on transaction size
explanation: The left side of the cash book layout format is the debit side and shows all the receipts of cash, whereas the right side shows all the payments.
subject: Accountancy

question: Why are the individual accounts appearing on the left (receipts) side of the Cash Book credited when posted to their respective ledger accounts?
options: Because expenses are increasing inside the firm
Because cash has been received in respect of them, creating a credit effect in those corresponding accounts
Because it reduces the capital account balance implicitly
To prepare a non-cash transfer voucher entry
explanation: The accounts appearing on the debit (receipts) side of the cash book are credited in their respective ledger accounts because cash has been received in respect of them, satisfying the double-entry rule.
subject: Accountancy

question: Cash payments made to suppliers or for expenses are entered on which side of a single column cash book?
options: The left side in the Receipts column
The right side in the Payments column
In the L.F. folio column directly
Only inside the Journal Proper daybook
explanation: All cash payments are recorded on the right side (Credit side) of the cash book under the Payments column.
subject: Accountancy

question: Why do modern business organizations sub-divide a cash book into a Double Column Cash Book format?
options: To separate trade discounts from cash discounts columns
To handle large volumes of bank transactions directly alongside cash transactions
To track employee working hours logs rows
To record the credit purchases of raw materials
explanation: Now-a-days bank transactions are very large in number. Having a separate amount column for bank transactions inside the cash book itself (Double Column Cash Book) helps in tracking bank positions easily alongside cash.
subject: Accountancy

question: What is the standard banking practice regarding interest on current accounts opened by businessmen?
options: Banks pay maximum compound interest quarterly.
Banks do not allow any interest on the balance in a current account.
Banks pay interest only if the balance exceeds millions.
Interest is paid in the form of extra blank cheque books.
explanation: A businessman generally opens a current account with a bank. Banks do not allow any interest on the balance in a current account but charge a small service amount.
subject: Accountancy

question: What are the small fees charged periodically by commercial banks for rendering checking services on a current account called?
options: Premium margins
Incidental charges
Interest credits
Voucher balances
explanation: Banks charge a small amount, called incidental charges, for the banking services rendered to a current account holder.
subject: Accountancy

question: What standard form must be filled out by a customer to deposit physical cash or cheques into their bank account?
options: Cheque slip
Pay-in-slip
Debit Note
Transfer voucher card
explanation: For depositing cash/cheques in the bank account, a form has to be filled, which is called a pay-in-slip.
subject: Accountancy

question: What component of a pay-in-slip form is signed by the cashier and returned to the depositor as an acknowledgment receipt?
options: The main ledger sheet
The counterfoil
The blank check leaf
The transfer token row
explanation: A pay-in-slip contains a counterfoil which is detached, signed by the bank cashier, and returned to the customer as a deposit receipt.
subject: Accountancy

question: In case of a cheque crossed as 'A/c payee only', how can the amount of that cheque be recovered?
options: It can be cashed immediately over the bank counter in physical currency.
It can be deposited only into the bank account of the person whose name appears on the cheque.
It can be transferred to any stranger by simple delivery.
It must be converted into a cash memo by a merchant.
explanation: In case of an A/c payee only crossing, the amount of the cheque cannot be paid as cash across the counter; it can be deposited only in the account of the person whose name appears on the cheque.
subject: Accountancy

question: When the name of a specific bank is explicitly written between two parallel lines drawn on a cheque leaf, what type of crossing is formed?
options: General crossing
Special crossing
Endorsed crossing
Bearer crossing token
explanation: When the name of the bank is written between two parallel lines on a cheque, it becomes a special crossing and the payment can be made only through that specific bank named.
subject: Accountancy

question: How can an uncrossed order cheque be transferred by its payee to another person legally?
options: By mere physical delivery to the new person without signature
By endorsement and delivery
By shredding the counterfoil receipt
It can never be transferred under any legal rule
explanation: A bearer cheque can be passed on by mere delivery. An order cheque, however, can be transferred to another person by endorsement and delivery.
subject: Accountancy

question: What does the process of 'Endorsement' of a cheque explicitly mean?
options: Depositing the cheque leaf into a wooden lockbox drop box
Writing instructions to pay the cheque to a particular person and signing it on the back of the cheque
Erasing the bank name written between parallel cross lines
Matching voucher dates chronologically
explanation: Endorsement means the writing of instructions on the back of the cheque to pay its value to a particular person, followed by signing it.
subject: Accountancy

question: What does writing the letter 'C' inside the L.F. column of a Double Column Cash Book signify?
options: A credit purchase adjustment that requires ledger posting
A contra entry, indicating that both cash and bank aspects are recorded here and no ledger posting is needed
A cash memo tracking serial number validation tag
A closing capital deficit warning code
explanation: Against entries involving transactions between cash and bank columns, the word C, which stands for contra, is written in the L.F. column indicating that these entries are completely recorded within the cash book and are not to be posted to the ledger.
subject: Accountancy

question: What does a credit balance appearing inside the bank column of a cash book indicate?
options: Vast surplus cash lying idle in bank lockers
A bank overdraft taken from the bank
An accounting entry balancing calculation error
Total value of trade discounts earned
explanation: Unlike the cash column (which always shows a debit balance), the bank column can show a credit balance. This indicates a bank overdraft taken from the bank.
subject: Accountancy

question: What is a bank overdraft situation formally defined as in the text?
options: When cash deposits exceed the safe limits set by state tax laws
A situation when cash withdrawn from the bank exceeds the amount of deposit
When a cheque leaf gets damaged or wet during transfer operations
When interest earned matches the incidental service charges
explanation: Overdraft is a situation when cash withdrawn from the bank account exceeds the total amount of deposit available in that account.
subject: Accountancy

question: If a cheque is received from a customer today but deposited into the bank on a completely different day, how is it recorded on the date of receipt?
options: It is directly entered into the bank column on the debit side.
It is treated as cash received and recorded in the cash column on the receipts side.
It is ignored entirely until the physical bank deposit day arrives.
It is recorded straight into the Journal Proper as a fixed asset.
explanation: If a cheque is deposited on a later day, on the date of receipt it is treated as cash received and recorded in the cash column on the receipts side. On the day of deposit, a contra entry moves it from cash to bank.
subject: Accountancy

question: If a cheque previously received from a customer and deposited in the bank is returned as 'dishonoured', how is it entered in the cash book?
options: The amount is added to the cash column on the receipt side.
The customer's account is debited by entering the amount in the bank column on the payment (credit) side.
The whole cash book page must be erased or canceled.
It is adjusted inside the Petty Cash Book system columns.
explanation: If a cheque received from a customer is dishonoured, the bank column of the cash book must be credited (on the payments side) against that customer's account name to reverse the previous deposit entry.
subject: Accountancy

question: Under what specialized system does a petty cashier normally manage funds and record small expenses in an organization?
options: Double-entry ledger indexing system
The Imprest system
Cash basis single row model
Accrual matrix promotion logic
explanation: To avoid overburdening the main cash book, large organizations appoint a petty cashier who operates strictly under the Imprest system.
subject: Accountancy

question: What is the fixed definite sum of money allocated to a petty cashier at the beginning of an accounting period called?
options: Incidental cash balance
Imprest amount
Voucher ceiling limit
Drawings allowance fund
explanation: Under the imprest system, a definite sum (say, 2,000) is given to the petty cashier at the start of a period. This amount is known as the imprest amount.
subject: Accountancy

question: Which of the following transaction groups is ideally suited for recording inside a Petty Cash Book?
options: Purchase of heavy factory machinery on long-term credit
Small repetitive payments like conveyance, cartage, postage, and telegrams
Inflow of capital funds introduced by partnership owners
Bulk export sales turnover revenue receipts
explanation: In every organization, a large number of small payments such as conveyance, cartage, postage, and telegrams are made. These small, repetitive items are managed via the petty cash book.
subject: Accountancy

question: What is the primary benefit or purpose of utilizing a separate Petty Cash Book system in business operations?
options: To bypass tracking miscellaneous costs completely
To prevent the main cash book from becoming too bulky and save the main cashier from being overburdened
To automatically eliminate trade discount calculations from ledger entries
To calculate bank overdraft values using separate indices columns
explanation: If all small payments are handled in the main cash book, the procedure becomes cumbersome, bulky, and overburdens the main cashier. A petty cash book efficiently handles this load.
subject: Accountancy

question: How are the totals of individual analytical expense columns of a petty cash book periodically posted to the ledger accounts?
options: Each small transaction row is posted separately every single day.
The total of each expense column is debited to its respective expense account in the ledger at the end of the period.
They are credited directly to the Capital account index.
They are moved straight into the Purchases Return book columns.
explanation: At the end of the month or period, the totals of various analytical expense columns are extracted and debited to their respective individual accounts (like Postage A/c or Cartage A/c) in the main ledger.
subject: Accountancy

question: What type of business transactions are strictly recorded inside a specialized Purchases (Journal) Book?
options: All cash and credit purchases of assets and properties
All credit purchases of goods or merchandise meant for resale
Only the cash purchases of office stationary items
Return of damaged goods back to our suppliers
explanation: The Purchases Book is a special journal used to record only credit purchases of goods in which the firm deals. Cash purchases and purchases of assets are excluded from this book.
subject: Accountancy

question: What is the primary source document utilized by an accountant to enter records into the Purchases Book?
options: Cash memo ticket issued to a client
Invoice or bill received from the supplier of goods
Debit Note sent to a trade partner
Passbook page credit confirmation
explanation: The source documents for recording entries in the purchases journal are the inbound invoices or bills received by the firm from its suppliers.
subject: Accountancy

question: How is a trade discount treated when entering item figures inside a Purchases Book line?
options: It is recorded separately as an expense account debit entry.
It is deducted from the gross invoice price, and only the net amount is recorded in the purchases book.
It is added to the asset value calculation column.
It is ignored entirely and the gross book price is entered.
explanation: Trade discounts allowed by suppliers are deducted from the gross invoice list price directly on the face of the bill, and the net purchase amount is entered in the subsidiary book.
subject: Accountancy

question: Why is the purchase of an office computer for cash completely excluded from the Purchases Book?
options: Because it contains no trade discount parameters.
Because the Purchases Book tracks only credit purchases of merchandise goods, not cash transactions or fixed asset acquisitions.
Because computers are treated as intangible items under GAAP rules.
Because it must be entered inside the Petty Cash Book instead.
explanation: The purchase of a computer for cash is a cash payment transaction affecting an asset, so it belongs in the Cash Book, not the Purchases Book (which is solely for credit purchases of trading goods).
subject: Accountancy

question: What transactions are recorded inside a specialized Purchases Return (Journal) Book?
options: Return of goods sold to customers for immediate cash
Return of goods or trading merchandise previously purchased on credit to suppliers
Return of old fixed assets rejected by factory floors
Allowance modifications resulting from cash discounts
explanation: The purchases return journal is used to record the return of goods or trading merchandise back to suppliers that were originally bought on credit terms.
subject: Accountancy

question: What specific document is prepared as the primary source of reference for entering records into the Purchases Return Book?
options: Credit Note received from an outside client
Debit Note prepared and sent to the supplier
Cash memo copy ticket
Pay-in-slip bank receipt counterfoil
explanation: When goods are returned to a supplier, a Debit Note is prepared and sent to them, which serves as the source document for the entry in the Purchases Return Book.
subject: Accountancy

question: What does a standard Debit Note contain to satisfy the Objectivity principle when returning goods to a supplier?
options: The global bank clearance identification numbers
The name of the party, details of goods returned, and the specific reason for return
The current market replacement value index of the assets
A formal signature from government regulatory auditors
explanation: A debit note contains the name of the supplier to whom goods are returned, item descriptions, quantities, values, and reasons for debiting their account.
subject: Accountancy

question: Which business transactions are systematically logged inside a standard Sales (Journal) Book?
options: All cash and credit sales of real properties and land
Only the credit sales of trading goods or merchandise to customers
All cash collection receipts from accounts debtors
Goods returned back by our customers
explanation: The Sales Book is a subsidiary daybook used exclusively for recording credit sales of merchandise goods in which the firm deals. Cash sales are entered in the Cash Book.
subject: Accountancy

question: What is the primary source document issued by a business to record transactions in its Sales Book?
options: Inbound bill invoice received from vendors
Outbound invoice or bill issued to the customer
Credit Note issued following an inspection
Voucher slip from a petty cashier box
explanation: The source documents for entering transactions in the sales journal are copy files of outbound invoices or bills issued by the firm to its customers.
subject: Accountancy

question: If an item of inventory is sold to an outside firm for immediate check payment, where is this transaction recorded?
options: Sales Book
Cash Book (Bank column)
Journal Proper
Petty Cash Book
explanation: Since it involves immediate check payment received by bank columns, it constitutes a cash/bank receipt and belongs in the Cash Book, not the credit Sales Book.
subject: Accountancy

question: Where is the periodic total sum of the credit entries from a Sales Book posted in the main ledger?
options: Debited to the Sales account
Credited to the Sales account
Credited directly to the Capital account
Debited to the Purchases account
explanation: At periodic intervals (usually monthly), the total of the sales book column is transferred and credited to the Sales Account in the principal ledger.
subject: Accountancy

question: What type of transactions are recorded inside a specialized Sales Return (Journal) Book?
options: Return of trading goods previously sold to customers on credit terms
Return of machinery elements rejected by manufacturing plants
Cash refunds allowed for defective office storage folders
Goods sent on approval lines to external partners
explanation: The sales return journal (or return inwards book) records transactions concerning trading merchandise returned back by buyers who had bought them on credit.
subject: Accountancy

question: What is the primary source document utilized to write transaction records into the Sales Return Book?
options: Debit Note prepared by our billing staff
Credit Note issued to the customer returning the goods
Outbound invoice bill receipt copy
Pay-in-slip bank deposit counterfoil
explanation: The source document for entries in the sales return book is a Credit Note issued to the customer, acknowledging their return and reducing their account balance.
subject: Accountancy

question: What is the primary reason an organization issues a Credit Note to a customer?
options: To demand immediate payment of an overdue bill
To inform them that their outstanding debt balance is being credited (reduced) because they returned damaged goods
To grant them an extra credit term period length
To document an internal transfer event between departments
explanation: A credit note is sent to a customer to let them know that their account has been credited (reduced) because the goods they sent back have been accepted by the business.
subject: Accountancy

question: What is the primary purpose of maintaining a Journal Proper in sub-divided bookkeeping systems?
options: To track cash and bank inflows/outflows rows
To record special and miscellaneous transactions that cannot fit into any other subsidiary book
To bypass double-entry balancing parity controls
To keep a summary record of all petty cash vouchers
explanation: The Journal Proper is a residual book where entries that do not fit into any other special subsidiary book (like cash book, purchases book, or sales book) are recorded.
subject: Accountancy

question: Which of the following transaction categories is natively recorded inside the Journal Proper?
options: Cash received from a customer debtor
Opening entries, closing entries, adjustment entries, and rectification entries
Credit sales of trading goods to clients
Small conveyance charges paid by a petty cashier
explanation: The Journal Proper handles specialized architectural entries including: opening entries, closing entries, transfer entries, adjustment entries, and rectification entries.
subject: Accountancy

question: If a business enterprise buys office furniture worth 25,000 on credit from a supplier, in which book must this transaction be recorded?
options: Purchases Book
Cash Book
Journal Proper
Sales Book
explanation: Since it is a credit purchase of a fixed asset (Furniture) rather than trading goods, it cannot go into the Purchases Book. Since no cash changes hands, it belongs in the Journal Proper.
subject: Accountancy

question: Which categories of ledger accounts are periodically balanced and have their remaining balances carried forward to the next period?
options: Nominal accounts handling expenses and revenues
Real accounts (assets) and Personal accounts (liabilities and capital)
Only the trade discount and drawings accounts rows
Only the cash memos voucher entries columns
explanation: Accounts in the ledger are periodically balanced. Real accounts (representing assets) and Personal accounts (representing liabilities or capital) are balanced, and their values are carried forward via 'Balance c/d'.
subject: Accountancy

question: How are Nominal accounts (revenues and expenses) handled at the close of an accounting period instead of being balanced and carried forward?
options: They are deleted from the system ledger pages completely.
They are closed by transferring their period totals straight to the Trading and Profit and Loss account.
They are added directly to the non-current fixed assets column.
They are converted into contra entries using the symbol 'C'.
explanation: Nominal accounts (handling items like salaries, rent, or sales) are not balanced and carried forward; instead, they are closed at the end of the year by moving their cumulative figures to the Trading and Profit and Loss account to calculate profit.
subject: Accountancy
